examples.title: Tree Node Structure Definition
examples.description: Definition of a basic tree node structure with fields for data and children
examples.code:
STRUCTURE TreeNode
  DATA item
  TreeNode firstChild    // Pointer to first child
  TreeNode nextSibling  // Pointer to next sibling
END STRUCTURE

// For Binary Tree specifically
STRUCTURE BinaryTreeNode
  DATA item
  BinaryTreeNode left   // Pointer to left child
  BinaryTreeNode right  // Pointer to right child
END STRUCTURE
examples.explanation: This defines the basic node structure for trees. The first structure shows a general tree node with first child and next sibling pointers. The binary tree structure has explicit left and right child pointers. The sibling pointer allows representing general trees using binary tree representation.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per node - just stores pointers and data

examples.title: Calculate Height of a Tree
examples.description: Recursive algorithm to find the height of a tree by computing maximum depth
examples.explanation: The height of a tree is computed recursively by finding the maximum height of left and right subtrees and adding 1 for the current node. Base case returns -1 for empty tree (or 0 depending on definition). This follows post-order traversal.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) - Recursion stack depth equals tree height, where h is height

examples.title: Binary Tree Implementation in Python
examples.description: Practical implementation of binary tree node and basic operations
examples.explanation: This Python implementation shows how to create a binary tree with nodes containing data and left/right pointers. The count_nodes function demonstrates tree traversal, while find_depth shows how to calculate the depth of any specific node by recursively searching.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for both functions - visit every node
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) worst case for recursion stack, O(h) average for balanced tree
